story:
God selected Earth—a tiny speck in a remote galaxy—as a secret laboratory for experimenting before His public performances elsewhere. After creating days, nights, plants, and animals, He formed Adam from dust as a miniature image of Himself and placed him in Eden.

When Adam failed to communicate with the animals and became mentally unstable, God cloned a companion from Adam's rib. Adam named him Steve. Both beings were genderless, uneducated, and ill-mannered, constantly fighting over rocks and stepping in animal waste. Their terrible hygiene made Eden unbearable.

To remedy this, God devised a plan: He planted a fruit tree with "educational hormones" in Eden's center, gave a snake the ability to speak, and explicitly forbade the pair from eating the fruit—expecting reverse psychology to work. However, God had forgotten to make them like fruit. Being carnivores, they killed and ate the snake instead.

Correcting His oversight, God made Steve crave fruit. After eating apples, Steve's body transformed—developing breasts and new anatomy. Jealous, Adam also ate the fruit and developed male characteristics. During their subsequent intimate encounter, Adam moaned "Eve" instead of "Steve" because it was easier to vocalize, and the name stuck.

Eve birthed three sons: Cain, Seth, and Abel. With no females being born,  Mama Eve became their only other option. God grew concerned about the species' future. God waved the humans out of Eden to make them all too busy to breed while He figured out a new plan. While he thought and thought, humans replicated through intense inbreeding, inexplicably.

Early humans lived extraordinarily long—Adam and Eve reached 930 years, and descendants averaged 904 years. After searching for over a millennium, God found Noah, a 602-year-old drunk whom God chose to build an ark.

The flood, according to witness Oscar Genesis, was merely a distraction. God's true purpose was to grab the headlines and divert attention from the declining-lifespan adjustment God was quietly imposing on Earth under the cover of invisible omnipresence.

God's lifespan reduction worked on most creatures but missed the Greenland shark hiding under polar ice and the immortal jellyfish. From Abraham (175 years) to Moses (120 years), human lives shortened dramatically.

And, of course, through the following centuries, human lifespans got even briefer. Nevertheless, longevity being longevity, it took another eight-hundred years, after the flood, for God to get the scourge of longevity to completely die off, so to speak, to His approved level. 

God never explained what that level was or why He wanted life-spans to diminish. Did we mention God works in mysterious ways? It’s infernally unclear.

With modern lifespans now increasing again, God is considering meteors, super-volcanoes, or black holes—but has decided to try McDonald's first.

Oscar Genesis, the witness to all these events, was struck by lightning and died. Amen.
